title: Sudden collapse without warning in a polypharmacy patient

chart.oneLiner: 78M with CAD/prior inferior MI, hypertension, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, CKD, and early dementia presents after a sudden loss of consciousness with NO warning, on a background of recurrent near-fainting and fatigue — recently started on diltiazem and digoxin for rate control while already on metoprolol and donepezil.

opening: I just dropped — no warning at all. One second I was up making tea, then I woke up on the floor.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 78-year-old man, now alert but fatigued and a little forgetful about exact dates. Cooperative; answers what is asked but does not volunteer.

HPI: ABRUPT loss of consciousness with NO prodrome (no nausea, no warmth, no lightheadedness, no palpitations beforehand). He was standing in the kitchen, not straining or turning his head. Quick recovery within a minute or two, no confusion afterward, no tongue-biting, no incontinence, no jerking witnessed by his wife who was in the next room. Over the last two to three weeks he has had several episodes of near-fainting, worsening fatigue and exertional tiredness, and his home blood-pressure cuff keeps reading a 'slow pulse' in the 30s-40s. NO chest pain now, but if asked specifically he did have a few minutes of mild chest pressure two days ago that he ignored. NO shortness of breath at rest. The complete absence of any warning before the collapse is the key red flag for a cardiac (conduction) cause.
MEDICATION CHANGES (reveal only when asked about meds/recent changes): an outside cardiologist started DILTIAZEM and DIGOXIN about ten days ago to control his atrial fibrillation, and he was told to keep taking his METOPROLOL as well — so he is now on THREE drugs that slow the heart. He is not sure he was told to stop anything. He has not had labs checked since the new drugs were started.
PMH: hypertension; coronary artery disease with a prior INFERIOR MI (the inferior wall and AV node share the right coronary supply); paroxysmal atrial fibrillation; chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine he recalls as 'about 1.4'); early dementia (mild). MEDS: metoprolol succinate, diltiazem (new), digoxin (new), lisinopril, atorvastatin, aspirin, apixaban, donepezil. ALLERGIES: none. SOCIAL: former smoker (quit 20 years ago), rare alcohol, lives at home with his wife who helps manage his pills. FAMILY: noncontributory. ROS: fatigue, near-syncope, the one collapse; denies fevers, denies melena/bleeding, denies focal weakness or speech trouble.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: alert, fatigued-appearing, well-perfused but pale.
- Vitals: BP 98/60, HR ~36 and regular, RR 16, 36.8°C, SpO2 97% RA.
- Cardiac: profoundly BRADYCARDIC and REGULAR; intermittent CANNON A-waves in the JVP; variable intensity of S1 (signs of AV dissociation) when specifically examined; no new murmur.
- Neuro: alert, oriented to person and place, no focal motor or sensory deficit, no pronator drift.
- Skin/perfusion: warm peripheries, capillary refill ~2 seconds.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Emphasize (only if asked about prodrome/warning) that there was NO warning before the collapse.
- Reveal the recent diltiazem + digoxin start, and that he is still on metoprolol, ONLY when asked about medications or recent medication changes.
- Reveal the prior inferior MI and the brief chest pressure two days ago only when asked about cardiac history / chest symptoms.
- The bradycardia, cannon a-waves, and variable S1 are revealed only if the relevant exam is performed.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.Glucose: Fingerstick glucose 102 — normal; not a hypoglycemic cause of collapse.
labs.Troponin: High-sensitivity troponin mildly elevated at 0.06 ng/mL (ref <0.04) and essentially STABLE on a repeat — a small bump consistent with demand/old inferior disease and renal retention, NOT a clear acute STEMI pattern.
labs.BMP: Na 137, K 5.6 (MILD HYPERKALEMIA, can worsen AV conduction), Cl 102, HCO3 22 — anion gap = 137 - (102 + 22) = 13 (normal). BUN 38, Cr 1.9 (up from a baseline ~1.4 = AKI on CKD), Mg 2.0 normal, Ca normal.
labs.Digoxin: Serum digoxin level 2.6 ng/mL — SUPRATHERAPEUTIC (therapeutic 0.5-2.0); the new AKI has reduced clearance and the mild hyperkalemia is consistent with digoxin effect.
labs.TSH: Normal — does not explain the bradycardia.
labs.Echo: Echo (later): prior inferior wall motion abnormality, preserved-to-mildly-reduced EF (~45%), no critical valve lesion and no large effusion — structural syncope (severe AS) is excluded.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Third-degree (complete) AV block: regular P waves at ~80/min march through independently of a slow, regular ventricular escape at ~36/min; the PR intervals are completely variable (AV dissociation with the atrial rate FASTER than the ventricular rate). No acute ST-elevation MI pattern.
ekg.verify: From the LITFL 'Complete Heart Block (3rd degree AV block)' library page — download an example tracing that matches: third-degree AV block with AV dissociation, atrial rate faster than a slow ventricular escape; self-host with credit to litfl.com.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: Third-degree (complete) AV block: P waves and QRS complexes fully dissociated; atrial rate faster than and independent of a slow ventricular escape rhythm.
searchTerms: complete heart block 3rd degree AV block 12-lead ECG
ekg.clinicalVignette: An elderly patient on multiple AV-nodal-blocking drugs presents with profound fatigue, near-syncope, and a sudden collapse without warning.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Mildly enlarged cardiac silhouette (cardiothoracic ratio just over 0.5) on an adequate PA film; lungs clear with no pulmonary edema, no effusion, no pneumothorax — no acute cardiopulmonary cause for the collapse.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: borderline cardiomegaly on an adequate PA film with otherwise clear lungs and no edema. Self-host with appropriate credit.
cxr.imageDescription: PA chest radiograph showing borderline cardiomegaly with clear lungs
searchTerms: enlarged cardiac silhouette clear lungs radiograph
cxr.clinicalVignette: An elderly man with longstanding hypertension and CAD has a chest film to assess heart size and exclude pulmonary edema.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this syncope (note your leading diagnosis). In your answer, name at least THREE distinct etiologies/mechanisms that can produce high-grade or complete AV block in this specific patient.
steps.rubric: Cardiac syncope from high-grade / complete AV block (leading & CANT-MISS,7). Names ≥3 mechanisms/etiologies of AV block in THIS patient — intrinsic age-related conduction-system (Lev/Lenegre) disease; ischemic AV block from prior/ongoing inferior (RCA) MI; AV-nodal-blocking DRUG toxicity (metoprolol + diltiazem + supratherapeutic digoxin); hyperkalemia / metabolic; high vagal tone (core,6, ~2 each). Drug-induced bradycardia from triple AV-nodal blockade as a genuine competing cause (CANT-MISS,4). Tachyarrhythmia / VT (CANT-MISS,2). Structural disease / aortic stenosis (core,1). Vasovagal (much less likely — NO prodrome) (core,1). Recognizes abrupt syncope without prodrome + bradycardia points to a conduction problem and that the medication list and renal function are central confounders (core,4). PENALTY: anchoring on a single cause without addressing the polypharmacy/ischemia/electrolyte confounders (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to high-grade / complete (third-degree) AV block. THREE-plus mechanisms that can cause AV block in THIS man: (1) intrinsic age-related conduction-system (Lev/Lenegre) degeneration; (2) ischemic AV block — his prior inferior (RCA-territory) MI and the brief chest pressure two days ago, since the RCA supplies the AV node; (3) AV-nodal-blocking DRUG effect/toxicity — he is on metoprolol PLUS newly added diltiazem PLUS digoxin that is now supratherapeutic; (4) metabolic — mild hyperkalemia worsening conduction. The strongest competing cause is drug-induced bradycardia from triple AV-nodal blockade layered on new AKI. Must-not-miss alternatives: a tachyarrhythmia (VT) and structural disease / severe aortic stenosis. Vasovagal is much less likely given NO prodrome. The absence of any warning plus bradycardia points to a conduction problem, and the medication list and renal function are central confounders that must be reconciled.

criticalActions.item: Cardiac syncope due to high-grade / complete AV block
Drug-induced bradycardia from triple AV-nodal blockade (metoprolol + diltiazem + supratherapeutic digoxin) as a competing cause

coreActions.item: Names ≥3 mechanisms/etiologies of AV block (intrinsic conduction disease, ischemic/inferior MI, drug toxicity, hyperkalemia, vagal)
Recognizes abrupt syncope without prodrome + bradycardia points to a conduction problem; flags the meds and renal function as central confounders

penalties.item: Anchors on a single cause without addressing the polypharmacy/ischemia/electrolyte confounders

mustNotMiss: Drug-induced bradycardia from triple AV-nodal blockade

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: EKG immediately (CANT-MISS — diagnostic,5). Continuous telemetry / cardiac monitoring (CANT-MISS,4). BMP including potassium and a creatinine for AKI (core,2). Serum DIGOXIN level given the new drug and renal impairment (core,2). Troponin for ischemia (core,1). TSH (core,0.5). CXR and Echo (core,0.5 combined). PENALTY: discharging or failing to place the patient on a monitor (-3); giving additional rate-control / AV-nodal blockers as 'workup' (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG immediately; place on continuous telemetry/cardiac monitoring; BMP (potassium + creatinine to capture the hyperkalemia and AKI); serum digoxin level (new drug + AKI); high-sensitivity troponin; TSH; CXR; and an echocardiogram. Critically, reconcile the medication list right away because three AV-nodal-blocking drugs plus a supratherapeutic digoxin level are part of the workup.

penalties.item: Discharging or failing to place the patient on a monitor

contraindicatedTests: Discharging or failing to place the patient on a monitor

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's OWN 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings (be specific about the rhythm) and state how it fits — or does not fit — the competing causes you raised.
steps.rubric: Findings = third-degree (complete) AV block: regular P waves marching independently at a rate FASTER than a slow, regular ventricular escape, with completely variable PR intervals (AV dissociation). CREDIT: identifies the bradycardia/slow ventricular rate (3); recognizes P waves and QRS are DISSOCIATED / independent with the atrial rate faster (6); names complete/third-degree AV block (3); notes NO acute STEMI pattern and that the dissociation is structural — drug toxicity and hyperkalemia may have UNMASKED or worsened it but the tracing itself defines complete block requiring pacing (2); systematic read (1). PENALTY: misreading as sinus bradycardia or 1st/2nd-degree block, or calling it 'just drug effect' that resolves with observation (-3).
keyFindings: P waves and QRS fully DISSOCIATED with the atrial rate FASTER than the ventricular rate
no acute STEMI pattern; recognizes drug/electrolyte effects may unmask but do not negate complete block
criticalFindings: Third-degree (complete) AV block: regular P waves at ~80/min march through independently of a slow regular ventricular escape at ~36/min, with completely variable PR intervals (AV dissociation, atrial rate faster than ventricular).
commonMistakes: misreading as sinus bradycardia or 1st/2nd-degree AV block
calling it 'just digoxin/diltiazem effect' that will resolve with observation rather than complete block needing pacing
steps.idealAnswer: Third-degree (complete) AV block: there is a regular atrial rhythm (P waves ~80/min) that is completely dissociated from a slow, regular ventricular escape (~36/min); the PR intervals vary randomly because no atrial impulse conducts. There is no acute ST-elevation MI pattern. This is the patient's own tracing and it explains his collapse — the escape rhythm is slow and unreliable. His three AV-nodal-blocking drugs, the supratherapeutic digoxin, and the mild hyperkalemia may have unmasked or worsened the block, but the EKG itself defines complete heart block that needs monitoring and pacing rather than mere observation.

criticalActions: Recognizes: third-degree (complete) AV block with AV dissociation — regular P waves marching independently, atrial rate faster than a slow regular ventricular escape.
coreActions: P waves and QRS fully dissociated, atrial rate faster than ventricular
no acute STEMI pattern; drug/electrolyte effects may unmask but do not negate complete block

penalties.label: misreads as sinus bradycardia / lower-grade block, or calls it transient drug effect that resolves with observation

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = borderline cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio just over 0.5) on an adequate PA film; lungs clear with NO pulmonary edema or effusion. CREDIT: identifies the enlarged cardiac silhouette / measures the cardiothoracic ratio (5); confirms an adequate PA projection and otherwise clear lungs with no edema as a deliberate pertinent negative (5). PENALTY: calling the film grossly abnormal / inventing pulmonary edema, or over-calling cardiomegaly on an AP/portable film without checking projection (-3).
keyFindings: borderline cardiomegaly on a PA film
clear lungs with no pulmonary edema
criticalFindings: Borderline cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio just over 0.5) with clear lungs and no pulmonary edema.
commonMistakes: calling a normal heart enlarged on an AP/portable film
inventing pulmonary edema on a clear film
steps.idealAnswer: Borderline cardiomegaly with a cardiothoracic ratio just over 0.5 on an adequate PA film; the lungs are clear with no pulmonary edema, effusion, or pneumothorax — no acute cardiopulmonary cause for the collapse and no congestion to argue against fluids if needed.

criticalActions: Borderline cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio just over 0.5) with clear lungs and no pulmonary edema.
coreActions: clear lungs with no pulmonary edema

penalties.label: calls the study grossly abnormal / invents pulmonary edema

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the EKG and the labs, commit to your diagnosis. Explain why this is dangerous, how you weighed the competing drug-toxicity and ischemic causes, and what single finding most discriminates complete heart block from a purely drug-induced bradycardia.
steps.rubric: Complete (third-degree) heart block causing cardiac syncope, committed from the patient's own EKG (5). Explains the danger — slow unreliable escape, risk of prolonged asystole / sudden death (3). Weighs the fork: the supratherapeutic digoxin, triple AV-nodal blockade, hyperkalemia, and prior inferior MI all CONTRIBUTE and must be reversed, but the EKG shows fixed AV dissociation = true complete block; the discriminating step is to remove/reverse the reversible drivers and confirm whether complete block PERSISTS, with EKG-proven AV dissociation (not just slowed conduction) the key feature favoring intrinsic complete block over pure drug effect (2).
steps.idealAnswer: Diagnosis: complete (third-degree) heart block causing cardiac syncope — committed from the patient's own EKG showing fixed AV dissociation with the atrial rate faster than a slow ventricular escape. It is dangerous because that escape rhythm is slow and unreliable, risking prolonged asystole, hemodynamic collapse, and sudden death; he needs monitoring and pacing now. Weighing the fork: the supratherapeutic digoxin (2.6) driven by new AKI, the triple AV-nodal blockade (metoprolol + diltiazem + digoxin), the mild hyperkalemia, and the prior inferior (RCA) MI are all genuine contributors and every reversible one must be corrected. The single most discriminating feature is the EKG itself — true AV DISSOCIATION (P waves marching independently through a regular escape) defines complete block, whereas a purely drug-induced bradycardia tends to slow conduction rather than fully dissociate; the discriminating maneuver is to stop and reverse the offending drugs/electrolytes and see whether complete block persists. Because reversibility is uncertain and the block may be fixed conduction-system disease, he cannot be assumed to recover with observation alone.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including disposition? Explicitly state whether this patient can be safely discharged and what must happen instead (medication reconciliation, monitoring, follow-up).
steps.rubric: Per MGH syncope / bradycardia / pacing: continuous telemetry monitoring (CANT-MISS,3); transcutaneous then transvenous pacing for symptomatic/unstable bradycardia (CANT-MISS,5); cardiology/EP consult for permanent pacemaker (CANT-MISS,4); STOP all AV-nodal-blocking agents — hold metoprolol, diltiazem, digoxin (CANT-MISS,3); atropine as a temporizing measure, acknowledging limited effect in infranodal block (2); treat hyperkalemia and the digoxin toxicity (consider digoxin-specific Fab if clinically toxic), correct AKI / hold nephrotoxic-renally-cleared drugs (2); explicit SAFE-DISCHARGE statement — patient CANNOT be discharged; admit to a monitored bed, do formal medication reconciliation with the wife/caregiver, arrange close cardiology follow-up and pacemaker plan (3). PENALTY: discharging complete heart block (-5); giving or continuing AV-nodal blockers / additional rate control (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Put him on continuous telemetry and admit to a monitored bed — he absolutely CANNOT be discharged. For symptomatic/unstable bradycardia: atropine as a temporizing measure (recognizing it often fails in infranodal/complete block), then transcutaneous pacing, then a temporary transvenous pacer as a bridge. STOP every AV-nodal-blocking agent immediately — hold metoprolol, diltiazem, and digoxin. Treat the reversible drivers: manage the mild hyperkalemia, address digoxin toxicity (digoxin-specific Fab fragments if clinically/hemodynamically toxic), and correct the AKI while holding renally-cleared/nephrotoxic agents. Obtain an urgent cardiology/EP consult for a permanent pacemaker, which is the definitive therapy if complete block persists after reversible causes are corrected. Safe-disposition plan: admit, perform a careful medication reconciliation WITH his wife/caregiver (the triple AV-nodal blockade is the avoidable error), educate them on which drugs were stopped and why, and arrange close cardiology follow-up and a clear pacemaker decision before any future discharge.

criticalActions.item: Cardiology / EP consult for permanent pacemaker
Stop all AV-nodal-blocking agents (hold metoprolol, diltiazem, digoxin)

coreActions.item: Atropine as a temporizing measure (limited effect in infranodal block)
Treat hyperkalemia and digoxin toxicity (digoxin-specific Fab if toxic); correct AKI
Safe-discharge statement: cannot discharge; admit to monitored bed, medication reconciliation with caregiver, close cardiology follow-up

bonusActions.item: Identify the recent triple AV-nodal blockade as the avoidable prescribing error and reconcile it

penalties.item: Giving or continuing AV-nodal blockers / additional rate control

requiredActions: Cardiology / EP consult for permanent pacemaker
Stop all AV-nodal-blocking agents (hold metoprolol, diltiazem, digoxin)
optionalActions: Atropine as a temporizing measure (limited effect in infranodal block)
Treat hyperkalemia and digoxin toxicity (digoxin-specific Fab if toxic); correct AKI
Safe-discharge statement: admit to monitored bed, medication reconciliation with caregiver, close cardiology follow-up
Identify the recent triple AV-nodal blockade as the avoidable prescribing error
unsafeActions: Giving or continuing AV-nodal blockers / additional rate control
steps.disposition: Admit; monitored bed (telemetry / CCU) with pacing capability

physicalExamMappings.finding: Cardiac: profoundly BRADYCARDIC and regular; intermittent CANNON A-waves in the JVP (signs of AV dissociation) when specifically examined.
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: JVP not elevated; no cannon a-waves.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Cardiac: bradycardic and regular with VARIABLE intensity of S1 (sign of AV dissociation); no new murmur.
Neuro: alert, oriented to person and place, no focal motor or sensory deficit, no pronator drift.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 78M with CAD/prior inferior MI, hypertension, paroxysmal AF, CKD, and early dementia presents after a sudden loss of consciousness with NO warning, on a background of recurrent near-fainting and fatigue. The diagnosis is Complete (third-degree) heart block, defined on his own EKG by fixed AV dissociation with the atrial rate faster than a slow ventricular escape. The picture is layered: he is on THREE AV-nodal-blocking drugs (metoprolol + newly added diltiazem + digoxin), the digoxin is supratherapeutic from new AKI, and he has mild hyperkalemia and a prior inferior (RCA) MI — all genuine contributors to the conduction block that must be reconciled and reversed even as the patient is stabilized and paced.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Cardiac: profoundly BRADYCARDIC and regular; intermittent CANNON A-waves in the JVP (signs of AV dissociation) when specifically examined.
Cardiac: bradycardic and regular with VARIABLE intensity of S1 (sign of AV dissociation); no new murmur.
Neuro: alert, oriented to person and place, no focal motor or sensory deficit, no pronator drift.
Skin/perfusion: warm peripheries, capillary refill ~2 seconds — adequately perfused despite the bradycardia.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals — abrupt syncope with no prodrome and profound bradycardia.
Elicit the focused history, eliciting the recent diltiazem/digoxin start, the persisting metoprolol, the prior inferior MI, and CKD only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam to reveal cannon a-waves and variable S1 (AV dissociation).

Build a differential led by complete AV block while naming ≥3 mechanisms (intrinsic conduction disease, ischemic inferior MI, drug toxicity, hyperkalemia) and weighing drug-induced bradycardia as the competing cause.
Order EKG and telemetry, send a digoxin level and BMP, and reconcile the medication list; interpret the patient's own EKG and the CXR.
Commit to complete heart block, reverse the drug/electrolyte/renal contributors, stabilize with pacing, and arrange safe disposition with medication reconciliation and a permanent-pacemaker plan.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on a single cause and ignoring the triple AV-nodal blockade, supratherapeutic digoxin, hyperkalemia, and AKI
Misreading the EKG as sinus bradycardia or lower-grade block
Assuming the block is purely drug-induced and will resolve with observation
managementPearls: Stop all AV-nodal-blocking agents and reconcile the medication list
Cardiology / EP consult for permanent pacemaker
criticalInterpretationItems: Third-degree (complete) AV block: regular P waves marching independently, atrial rate faster than a slow regular ventricular escape (AV dissociation).
Borderline cardiomegaly with clear lungs and no pulmonary edema.
criticalManagementItems: Cardiology / EP consult for permanent pacemaker
Stop all AV-nodal-blocking agents (hold metoprolol, diltiazem, digoxin)
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Third-degree (complete) AV block: regular P waves marching independently, atrial rate faster than a slow regular ventricular escape (AV dissociation).
Borderline cardiomegaly with clear lungs and no pulmonary edema.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Third-degree (complete) AV block: regular P waves marching independently, atrial rate faster than a slow regular ventricular escape (AV dissociation).
Borderline cardiomegaly with clear lungs and no pulmonary edema.
imageInterpretationPearls: Read the rhythm strip systematically: march out the P waves and the QRS complexes separately — independent regular atrial and ventricular rates with the atria faster define complete block.
AV-nodal-blocking drugs and hyperkalemia can unmask or worsen complete block, but the tracing still defines the rhythm and the need for pacing.
State pertinent negatives — no acute STEMI pattern, clear lungs with no edema — not just the positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern after a frightening collapse
Takes a careful medication history and confirms it with the caregiver
Explains next steps (monitoring, pacing, admission) in patient-friendly language

libraryUpgrade.imagePolicy: integrated patient-own EKG with pathology-forward CXR
